course_title: Professional Engineering and the Business Environment: Global Practice
course_description:
This course will explore the multifaceted landscape of international professional engineering practice. The goal of the course is to equip students with the knowledge and skills necessary to drive positive change and implement effective design solutions in real-world contexts.

Students will investigate business decision-making, economic drivers relevant to engineering projects, and key concepts required for professional practice within the sustainable development framework for projects in different countries. The course will strongly focus on how international and specific local contexts affect engineering projects in practice. Throughout the course, each student will contribute their knowledge and experience. Students will actively collaborate to produce engineering project proposals that account for stakeholder management, risk analysis, and sustainability considerations.

Students will explore the ethical responsibilities inherent in engineering practice as part of their individual development. Additionally, students will enhance their knowledge and skills through interactions with other team members.

This course is delivered through interactive, workshop-based learning built around real-world engineering project scenarios in diverse countries and organisational contexts. Students will work in diverse teams to select (or be assigned) a project context and iteratively develop a scoped project proposal through staged milestones, facilitated progress reviews, and structured peer feedback.

Weekly activities will emphasise practical professional engineering skills, including stakeholder identification and engagement planning, risk and uncertainty analysis, business case development (including basic financial reasoning and investment decision considerations), and sustainability framing using recognised principles and frameworks. The course also includes guided reflection on professional responsibility and ethical practice in situations involving competing stakeholder interests and long-term impacts.

Students are expected to contribute actively in class and in team settings, demonstrate professional conduct, and use feedback to enhance both the quality of their technical proposals and their professional practice capabilities.

incompatible: You can't enrol in this course if you've already completed the following:
restrictions: Enrolment Restricted to students in the GCEngSc, MEngSc and MEngSc(Man) in the field of Mechanical, Mechatronic or Materials and Manufacturing, MBioeng(Prof), MChemEng(Prof), MCivilEng(Prof), MElecEng(Prof), MMatManEng(Prof), MMechEng(Prof), MSoftEng(Prof), MUrbWatEng(Prof) and the ME program.

course_contacts.name: Professor Mohsen Yahyaei
course_contacts.notes: Professor Yahyaei will be available for consultation Mondays, from 14:00-16:00.

timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
course_aims: In this course, you will apply business decision-making processes relevant to engineering projects through practical case studies and real-world scenarios. You will develop project analysis skills, focusing on decision stages through to the final investment decision. This includes using standard financial analysis methods that determine expected returns on investment and can be used to quantify financial risks. The course will deepen your understanding of ethical professional engineering practice, with a focus on economic, social, environmental, and organisational considerations in engineering projects. You will develop your professional and interpersonal skills, communication and collaboration, by working in diverse teams.

learning_outcomes.description: Work effectively in diverse teams to deliver feasibility study reports and project pitch for an engineering project.
Develop engineering project scopes that align with project goals and stakeholder expectations.
Critically analyse and articulate the ethical responsibilities of engineers, specifically focusing on their duty to investors, clients, stakeholders, society, and future generations.
Evaluate engineering project risks associated with finance, logistics, policy, and other factors.
Identify key stakeholders for an engineering project.
Develop engagement plans to communicate effectively with diverse stakeholders of engineering projects from a global perspective.
Apply a suitable sustainable framework to an engineering project in a global context.
Evaluate and analyse economical feasibility and investment options, taking into consideration the costs, benefits, and sustainability of engineering project proposals.

assessment_details.title: Professionalism and Teamwork Engagement
assessment_details.due_date: 9/03/2026 - 29/05/2026 The draft team charter needs to be reviewed with the teaching team before submission. Submission due at 16:00 on 13 March 2026 Each team requires one submission. No individual submission is required. Peer assessment due by Monday of the following week after submission at 16:00.
assessment_details.other_conditions: Peer assessed. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Assessment 1 evaluates each student’s professionalism demonstrated through consistent participation, preparation, and constructive teamwork across the semester. Weekly workshops include structured activities that support the development of sequence reports and the final report, including briefings, Q&A sessions, progress reviews, and targeted feedback from the teaching team and invited faculty. Students are expected to engage professionally by: attending and participating actively in workshop activities and team progress discussions; demonstrating preparation and contribution to team tasks and deliverables; communicating respectfully and constructively with peers and staff; responding appropriately to feedback and managing team processes (e.g., planning, document control, meeting discipline, and conflict resolution). A required component of this assessment is the development and use of a Team Charter , which should be completed using the provided template. The Team Charter outlines agreed-upon roles, objectives, communication processes, document management procedures, and conflict resolution approaches. Students are expected to utilise the charter throughout the semester to support effective team operations and the delivery of project work. Professionalism is assessed individually based on observed engagement and conduct during workshops and scheduled team check-ins, as well as evidence of contribution to team processes (including use of the Team Charter). Where required, staff may ask students to explain or justify elements of their team processes during workshop interactions. Please refer to Blackboard for detailed marking criteria. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submit the Draft via Turnitin on Blackboard.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Extensions are available for the Team Charter submission only. Due to the ongoing nature of the remaining assessment (i.e., you are judged on your performance across the semester), there is no ability to request further extensions.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Viva Voce Interview: Individual Demonstration of Understanding (Hurdle Assessment)
assessment_details.due_date: 11/05/2026 - 29/05/2026 The schedule for Viva Voce Interviews will be provided two weeks in advance.
assessment_details.task_description: This assessment evaluates each student’s individual understanding of the project, independent of written team submissions. Each student will participate in a structured viva voce (oral) interview conducted by a member of the teaching team during Weeks 11–13. The interview assesses the student’s ability to explain, justify, and critically reflect on the financial, sustainability, and risk aspects of their team’s project, as well as their individual contribution. During the interview, students will be asked targeted questions relating to: the financial analysis undertaken (including key assumptions, discounted cash flow logic, and interpretation of results); sustainability considerations, stakeholder impacts, and ethical dimensions relevant to the project context; risk identification, mitigation strategies, and sensitivity to uncertainty; the student’s specific role and contribution within the team. The interview is designed to verify that students can demonstrate genuine comprehension, professional judgement, and applied reasoning , rather than reproducing prepared or generated material. Students are expected to articulate concepts clearly, respond to follow-up questions, and adapt explanations to new or hypothetical scenarios posed during the interview. This assessment is an individually secure assessment and includes a course hurdle requirement. Students must demonstrate a satisfactory level of individual understanding to pass the course, regardless of performance in team-based written assessments. Please refer to Blackboard for detailed marking criteria. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
No submission is required for this assessment. Students must attend their booked Interview Session on time.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
Discretionary extensions are not available for this task.
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
As this is timed-assessment, Student Access Plans (SAPs) and discretionary requests are not suitable supporting documentation for requesting an extension. Students must provide evidence that demonstrates their inability to attend the interview at their scheduled time.
Late submission
You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late .
The nature of the assessment does not allow for the application of a late penalty with a sliding scale, as they are conducted within the workshop timeslot at a pre-booked time.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension .
assessment_details.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late .

assessment_details.title: Sequence of Reports (Team Project Deliverables）
assessment_details.due_date: Report 1 (10%) 2/04/2026 6:00 pm Peer Assessment 1 2/04/2026 4:00 pm Report 2 (20%) 1/05/2026 4:00 pm Peer Assessment 2 5/05/2026 4:00 pm Final Report (30%) 9/06/2026 4:00 pm Peer Assessment 3 17/06/2026 4:00 pm Each team requires one submission. No individual submission is required. Each student must complete the Peer Assessment and submit their report.
assessment_details.task_description: Assessment 3 consists of a sequence of three project reports prepared by student teams. The sequence mirrors an authentic project development cycle, culminating in an integrated Final Investment Decision. Report 1 (Week 5; 10%) (LOs 2, 4, 8) focuses on scoping early-stage projects and conducting financial evaluations. Teams develop and justify a discounted cash flow (DCF) analysis, including key assumptions, baseline/counterfactual framing, and comparison of project options where applicable. Report 2 (Week 9; 20%) (LOs 3, 4, 5, 6, 7) extends the project by incorporating sustainability, stakeholder engagement, ethics, and project risk. Teams identify material impacts, propose mitigation and engagement strategies, and update project conclusions as appropriate. Final Report (Week 15 / exam period; 30%) (LOs 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8) integrates the work from Reports 1 and 2, incorporating feedback from the teaching team and insights gained from the oral presentation process. The Final Report must present a coherent, evidence-based recommendation and a Final Investment Decision , supported by transparent assumptions and analysis. Detailed requirements, submission format, and marking criteria are provided on Blackboard. Use of AI/MT tools: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description

Submission is via TurnItIn on Blackboard.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 7 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Extensions are limited to 7 days due to the sequential nature of this assessment. This will allow sufficient preparation for the Viva Voce and other subsequent assessment.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

learning_resources.library_resources: Library resources are available on the UQ Library website .

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (23 Feb - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: Theory sessions and discussions Intensive theory/discussion sessions on key principles in management, sustainability, stakeholder engagement, project development and financial analysis to enhance decision making in engineering projects and practice. Ethical considerations are prominent throughout the content. Academics and industry representatives will deliver key note and guest lectures.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (23 Feb - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: Workshop sessions Workshops are interactive, facilitated sessions that replace tutorials. They combine short briefings with guided activities where students apply course concepts to their project context, work in teams on staged deliverables, and receive formative feedback from the teaching team. Workshops also include structured peer review, progress checkpoints, and time for consultation to support professional communication, stakeholder thinking, risk analysis, and sustainability integration in the project proposal. Learning outcomes: L01

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (23 Feb - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: Assignments Working in small groups of nominally five or six students addressing problems and challenges which reflect the concepts covered in lectures to practice.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 11 To Week 13 (11 May - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: Viva Voce Interview: Individual Demonstration of Understanding (Hurdle Assessment) Evaluating each student’s individual understanding of the project, independent of written team submissions.

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure AI for Assessment Guide Recording of Teaching Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library .
